entries.title: Navigating the Tokyo Metro
entries.body: Tokyo's subway system is one of the world's most extensive, with 13 lines serving over 280 stations. Buy a Suica or Pasmo IC card at any station—these work on all trains, buses, and even convenience stores. The IC card costs ¥500 deposit and can be topped up at machines or via Apple Pay. Key lines for tourists: Yamanote Line (JR, loops around central Tokyo), Ginza Line (oldest, connects Asakusa to Shibuya), and Chuo/Sobu Line (east-west across the city). Always stand on the left side of escalators, and keep phone calls silent on the train.

entries.title: Essential Japanese Etiquette
entries.body: Japan has a rich culture of respect. Remove your shoes before entering a home or traditional restaurant. Bow slightly when greeting. Never stick chopsticks upright in rice. Slurping noodles is a compliment. Tipping is not practiced. Always carry cash—many places are cash-only. Trash cans are rare; carry your rubbish until you find one at a convenience store.

entries.title: Where to Eat in Shinjuku
entries.body: Shinjuku offers everything from Michelin-starred kaiseki to ¥500 ramen. Omoide Yokocho near the west exit has tiny yakitori stalls open late. Takashimaya Times Square B1-B2 has an excellent food hall. For ramen, try Ramen Street inside Shinjuku Station. Budget: street food ¥500-1,000 · casual ¥1,000-2,500 · mid-range ¥3,000-6,000.

entries.title: Senso-ji Temple, Asakusa
entries.body: Senso-ji is Tokyo's oldest temple, founded in 645 AD. Enter through the Kaminarimon ('Thunder Gate'). The Nakamise shopping street sells traditional souvenirs. Arrive before 8 AM to avoid crowds. Draw an omikuji (fortune) for ¥100. The five-storey pagoda and main hall are free to enter.

entries.title: Emergency Information
entries.body: Emergency numbers in Japan: Police 110 · Ambulance/Fire 119. Tokyo Metropolitan Police English helpline: 03-3501-0110 (24h). Japan Helpline (English): 0570-000-911. Hospitals with English services: St. Luke's International (Tsukiji): 03-5550-7166 · Tokyo Medical and Surgical Clinic (Toranomon): 03-3436-3028.
